title: The goldfish that quotes last month
content: AI memory can seem bizarre. The assistant remembers you are vegetarian from weeks ago, yet forgets the file you discussed an hour ago in the same conversation. It looks random, but it usually is not. There are distinct memory layers with different rules, and most strange behavior can be traced back to one of them.
next: Start with the strangest fact of all: the model itself.

title: The model remembers nothing
content: Between requests, an LLM retains no direct internal memory of the conversation. Each request is effectively a fresh start: it reads the text it is given, produces an answer, and does not carry that interaction forward by itself. This is why all useful 'memory' has to be supplied again through context.
next: The script has a name you already know.

title: Layer 1: the context window
content: The context window is everything the model sees for this one request: system prompt, conversation, documents, tool results. It is the whiteboard in front of the model - if something is on the whiteboard, the model 'knows' it; if not, it does not exist. And the whiteboard has a fixed size, measured in tokens.
next: So how does a conversation survive from one message to the next?

title: Layer 2: chat history, the memory illusion
content: When you send message five, the app often re-sends messages one through four along with it. The model reads the whole conversation again from scratch on each turn. That is the core memory illusion: not remembering, but re-reading. The experience feels continuous even though the model is just seeing old text again.
next: Re-reading works - until the stack gets too tall.

title: When the whiteboard fills up
content: A long conversation eventually outgrows the context window. Then the app must compromise: drop the oldest messages, or squash them into a short summary. Either way, detail is lost. This is exactly why an assistant deep into a marathon chat starts forgetting things from the beginning - the beginning is literally no longer in front of it.
next: But some facts survive across chats entirely. How?

title: Layer 3: saved memory
content: Some apps maintain actual notes. When you mention that you are vegetarian or that you have two kids, the app may extract that fact and store it in a database. In future chats it can insert those notes back into the context window. This is often what apps mean by a visible 'memory' feature.
next: One layer remains: knowledge too big for sticky notes.

title: Layer 4: fetched on demand
content: Your company's thousand documents cannot live on sticky notes or fit on a whiteboard. Instead, they sit in external storage, and when a question needs them, the relevant passages are searched out and placed into the context window just in time. This retrieval trick is important enough to get its own module.
next: Put the four layers together and the weirdness dissolves.

title: Every quirk, explained
content: Remembers you are vegetarian: saved memory. Forgot the start of a long chat: it fell out of the context window. A fresh chat knows nothing about yesterday: history belongs to each conversation unless the app carries it over. Knows your company handbook: it was fetched on demand. Once you separate the layers, the behavior becomes much easier to explain.
next: That fourth layer - finding the right document at the right moment - hides a beautiful idea.

title: The retrieval problem
content: Fetching the right passage sounds easy until you try it. The user asks 'can I bring my dog to the office?' and the answer lives in a document titled 'Workplace animal policy' that never contains the word 'dog... bring... office' in that order. Finding meaning, not just matching words - that requires a genuinely clever invention.
next: Next: embeddings and RAG - how AI searches by meaning.
